The main responsibility of children is to obey their parents. This obedience includes respect as well as actions, and throughout history, people who have obeyed their parents have experienced prosperity. Regarding the duty of parents: do not be impatient; avoid unreasonable punishments. Deal wisely with your children; convince them through reason and appeal to their understanding. Raise them properly, using gentle correction and teaching them what God requires. This duty is often neglected, even among those who claim to follow Jesus. Many discourage their children from religion; however, this does not excuse the children's disobedience, although it may be a cause of hardship. God alone can change a heart, yet he blesses good lessons and examples given by parents, and answers their prayers. But those whose main concern is that their children are wealthy and accomplished, regardless of their spiritual well-being, should not expect God’s blessing.

The duty of servants can be summarized in one word: obedience. Servants in the past were often slaves. The apostles were instructed to teach both servants and masters their duties, which would reduce injustice until slavery was eliminated through the influence of Christianity. Servants are to respect those who have authority over them. They should be honest; not pretending to obey when they intend to disobey, but serving faithfully. And they must serve their masters not only when being watched, but also diligently when their master is away. A consistent focus on Jesus Christ will make people faithful and sincere in every role, working willingly rather than under compulsion, because of a love for their employers and what they represent. This makes service pleasant for them, pleasing to their masters, and acceptable to the Lord Christ. God will reward even the most difficult work done out of a sense of duty and with the goal of glorifying him. Here is the duty of masters: act in the same way. Be fair to your servants, as you expect them to be to you; show kindness and concern for them, and strive to please God by doing so. Do not be harsh or overbearing. You have a Master to obey, and you and your servants are fellow-servants with respect to Christ Jesus. If masters and servants considered their duties to God and the account they will one day give to him, they would be more mindful of their duty to each other, leading to more orderly and happy families.

Spiritual strength and courage are necessary for our spiritual battle and suffering. Those who want to prove that they have true faith must strive for all aspects of grace and put on the full armor of God, which he provides. The Christian armor is meant to be worn; we cannot remove it until we finish fighting and complete our journey. The battle is not against human enemies or simply against our own sinful nature; we face an enemy who has many ways to deceive vulnerable souls. The devil attacks us in matters related to our spiritual lives, trying to erase the image of God from our hearts. We must resolve, by God’s grace, not to give in to Satan. Resist him, and he will flee. If we falter, he will gain ground. If we doubt either our cause or our leader or our armor, we give him an advantage. The different parts of the armor worn by heavily armed soldiers who faced intense enemy attacks are described here. There is no protection for the back; there is nothing to defend those who retreat in Christian warfare. Truthfulness and sincerity are like a belt, holding all the other pieces of armor together and being mentioned first. Without sincerity, there can be no true religion. The righteousness of Christ, credited to us, is a breastplate that protects against God’s wrath. The righteousness of Christ living within us strengthens our hearts against Satan's attacks. Resolve must be like greaves or leg armor; to stand firm and advance on difficult paths, we need the preparation of the gospel of peace. Motivation for obedience during trials comes from understanding the gospel. Faith is essential in times of temptation. Faith—trusting in unseen things, receiving Christ and his redemption, and thus gaining grace from him—is like a shield, defending us in every way. The devil is the enemy. Violent temptations that ignite the soul with hellfire are the darts Satan shoots at us. Also, negative thoughts about God and ourselves. Applying God’s word and Christ’s grace quenches those darts of temptation. Hope for salvation must be our helmet; a confident expectation of victory will purify the soul and keep it from being defiled by Satan. For the Christian armed for battle, the apostle recommends only one weapon: the sword of the Spirit, which is God's word. It subdues and controls sinful desires and blasphemous thoughts as they arise within us, and it refutes unbelief and error that attack us from outside. A single verse, well understood and applied correctly, can defeat a temptation or objection and overcome even the most formidable opponent. Prayer must bind together all parts of our Christian armor. There are other religious duties and responsibilities in this world, but we must maintain times of prayer. Even short, heartfelt prayers are appropriate when other tasks need to be done. We must have holy thoughts as part of our daily lives. A proud heart will be insincere in prayer. We must pray with all kinds of prayer: public, private, and secret; social and solitary; formal and spontaneous—with every aspect of prayer: confessing sin, asking for mercy, and giving thanks for blessings received. And we must do this by the grace of God the Holy Spirit, depending on him and following his guidance. We must persevere in specific requests despite discouragement. We must pray not only for ourselves but also for all believers. Our enemies are powerful, and we are weak, but our Redeemer is almighty, and through his power we can overcome. Therefore, let us encourage ourselves. Have we not often neglected to respond when God has called? Let us reflect on these things and continue our prayers with patience.

The gospel was a mystery until it was revealed by God; and it is the responsibility of Christ’s ministers to proclaim it. The most effective ministers need the prayers of believers. Those who face great hardship and danger in their work should be prayed for especially. May peace—meaning all kinds of peace: peace with God, peace of conscience, and harmony among yourselves—and the grace of the Spirit, producing faith and love, and every other blessing, be with those in whom these qualities have already begun. All grace and blessings come from God through Jesus Christ our Lord. Grace means God’s favor; all good things, both spiritual and material, are from him, and will be with all who sincerely love our Lord Jesus Christ.
